questions.scenario:
A build worker resolves `packages.example.net` to `203.0.113.25`, but downloads leave through an unexpected VPN. The worker reports:

```text
$ ip route get 203.0.113.25
203.0.113.25 via 10.90.0.1 dev tun0 src 10.90.12.8 uid 1000
```

Which conclusion should guide the next check?

options.text: DNS selected `tun0`, so the authoritative DNS zone must be changed to name the Ethernet interface
The destination is local to `10.90.12.8`, so the worker should ARP directly for `203.0.113.25`
The output proves the remote TLS certificate is invalid because the route uses a private source address
The operating system selected gateway `10.90.0.1`, interface `tun0`, and source `10.90.12.8`, so route priority and VPN policy should be reviewed before the application

questions.explanation: `ip route get` shows the route the kernel selected for this destination, including next hop, interface, and source address. DNS supplied only the destination IP. The public destination is not a local neighbor, and the evidence says nothing about TLS yet. [RFC 1122](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122) describes host IP routing responsibilities.

questions.scenario: A platform team needs an AWS subnet for 3,900 worker interfaces at peak. IPAM offers aligned `/22`, `/21`, `/20`, and `/19` blocks. The design must use the smallest block that fits after AWS reserves five addresses. Which block should be allocated?

options.text: `/21`, because its 2,048 total addresses are doubled when the subnet is private
`/20`, because 4,096 total addresses leave 4,091 usable after the provider reservations
`/19`, because AWS requires 8,192 addresses whenever a workload exceeds 1,000 interfaces
`/22`, because provider reservations replace the normal address-count formula

questions.explanation: IPv4 CIDR capacity is `2^(32 - prefix)`. A `/20` has 4,096 total addresses and 4,091 usable in an AWS subnet, making it the smallest offered block above 3,900. The `/21` and `/22` are too small, while `/19` wastes more of the parent allocation than required. [RFC 4632](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4632) defines CIDR, and [Amazon VPC subnet sizing](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/subnet-sizing.html) documents reserved addresses.

questions.scenario:
A vendor asks to peer a new analytics VPC with the company network. The routed inventory is:

| Network | CIDR |
| --- | --- |
| production | `10.0.0.0/16` |
| office VPN | `192.168.1.0/24` |
| vendor proposal | `10.0.128.0/17` |

What is the blocking design issue?

options.text: The vendor range is public because `/17` prefixes cannot be used with RFC 1918 addresses
The office VPN overlaps the vendor range because all private ranges are one routing domain
The vendor `/17` is contained inside production's `10.0.0.0/16`, so destination ownership would be ambiguous across the peering path
The ranges are safe because longest-prefix match automatically makes overlapping peered networks operationally unambiguous

questions.explanation: `10.0.128.0/17` is a subset of `10.0.0.0/16`. Networks that must route to each other need non-overlapping address space, even when both use valid private ranges. The office VPN does not overlap this proposal, and longest-prefix match cannot create unique ownership for duplicate destinations. [RFC 1918](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1918) defines private IPv4 space, while [RFC 4632](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4632) defines prefix routing.

questions.scenario: A payments company is moving a three-tier service into a VPC. Its draft places the internet-facing load balancer, application workers, and database in one subnet with `0.0.0.0/0 -> internet-gateway`. Workers need outbound package access, but the security requirement allows direct internet traffic only to the load balancer and requires the database to remain internal. Which subnet revision best meets those constraints?

options.text: Place the load balancer in public subnets, place workers in private subnets with outbound NAT, and keep database subnets on controlled internal routes
Keep every workload public and rely on application authentication as the only network boundary
Place the load balancer in database subnets and give the database the internet gateway route for health checks
Remove every default route because private workloads cannot initiate outbound internet connections through NAT

questions.explanation: Subnet role comes from routing and exposure, not merely from using a private IP. Public subnets support the internet-facing entry point, private app subnets can use NAT for outbound access, and database subnets should avoid direct internet routes. Keeping all tiers public expands the attack surface, while removing outbound paths can break required dependencies. [Amazon VPC route table documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Route_Tables.html) describes subnet route behavior.

questions.scenario:
A VPC route table contains these entries:

| Destination | Target |
| --- | --- |
| `10.0.0.0/16` | local |
| `10.0.32.0/20` | inspection appliance |
| `0.0.0.0/0` | NAT gateway |

A host sends a packet to `10.0.35.12`. Which route wins?

options.text: The default route, because it appears last and therefore overrides earlier rows
The local `/16`, because local routes always override more specific destinations
All three routes, because routers duplicate packets across every matching prefix
The `10.0.32.0/20` route, because it is the longest and therefore most specific matching prefix

questions.explanation: `10.0.35.12` matches all three prefixes, but `/20` is more specific than `/16` and `/0`. Route order does not replace longest-prefix matching, and matching routes do not cause packet duplication. [RFC 4632](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4632) explains classless prefix routing and aggregation.

questions.scenario: A platform team is approving an AWS network before deployment. The worker tier may reach 1,500 interfaces in `10.20.0.0/22`, while an application tier expected to reach 40 interfaces is assigned `10.20.16.0/27`. Each Availability Zone receives an ALB subnet sized `/28`, despite the service requirement for at least `/27` with eight free addresses. The environment must also route through a branch using `192.168.0.0/16` to a vendor advertising `192.168.40.0/24`. Which findings should block approval? Select all that apply.

options.text: The branch and vendor ranges are acceptable because private allocations may overlap when the VPN uses longest-prefix routing
The `/22` worker subnet is too small because it has only 1,024 total addresses before AWS reservations
The `/28` edge subnet is unsuitable for the ALB because AWS requires at least a `/27` and eight free addresses per Availability Zone subnet
The `/27` app subnet can support 40 interfaces if autoscaling launches them gradually rather than in one deployment
The branch `/16` contains the vendor `/24`, so direct VPN routing would have overlapping destination ownership

questions.explanation: The `/22` cannot fit 1,500 interfaces. The ALB proposal also fails its service-specific requirement: each Availability Zone subnet must be at least `/27` with eight free addresses. The vendor `/24` sits inside the branch `/16`, creating overlapping route ownership. The app `/27` cannot hold 40 interfaces after provider reservations. [Elastic Load Balancing subnet requirements](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/application-load-balancers.html#subnets-load-balancer) and [Amazon VPC subnet sizing](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/subnet-sizing.html) document these constraints.

questions.scenario:
A dual-stack service publishes both records:

```text
api.example.com. 300 IN A    198.51.100.25
api.example.com. 300 IN AAAA 2001:db8:10:20::25
```

IPv4 monitoring succeeds, but users on IPv6-preferred networks time out before TLS. Which response is most appropriate?

options.text: Delete the A record because publishing IPv4 prevents clients from using IPv6 routes
Rotate the certificate first because an IPv6 timeout proves the certificate lacks an AAAA extension
Test the IPv6 route, listener, and firewall policy because the AAAA record exposes a separate path that IPv4-only checks do not validate
Replace the `/64` subnet with `/24` because IPv6 uses the same prefix sizes as IPv4

questions.explanation: Publishing an AAAA record gives clients a distinct IPv6 path. IPv4 success does not prove the IPv6 route, firewall rules, load balancer listener, or required ICMPv6 behavior. TLS has not started in a connection timeout, and `/64` is a normal IPv6 subnet size rather than an IPv4-style error. [RFC 8200](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8200) specifies IPv6 behavior.

questions.scenario: A private application subnet was created as `/27` for 12 workers. Six months later, autoscaling requests 10 more workers, but deployments fail with `InsufficientFreeAddressesInSubnet`. Existing workers and the load balancer still serve traffic. What is the best long-term response?

options.text: Restart the existing workers because address exhaustion is an application memory leak
Add a correctly sized non-overlapping subnet and migrate or spread workloads, then update capacity planning because cloud subnets usually cannot be enlarged in place
Publish a public DNS record for every worker because public names create additional private addresses
Change the worker mask locally without changing the VPC allocation because hosts can claim adjacent cloud addresses independently

questions.explanation: The error identifies subnet address exhaustion, not an application or DNS problem. A host cannot safely expand the provider-managed CIDR by changing its own mask. The durable fix is additional planned address space and workload migration or distribution, followed by capacity controls that include autoscaling and provider reservations. [Amazon VPC subnet sizing](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/subnet-sizing.html) documents subnet address constraints.
